content:
# Exodus Chapter 31 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 100-101; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 74-75 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_031_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_031_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE — THE SPIRIT, THE SABBATH, AND THE FINGER OF GOD:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 31 across two folio pages in standard three-column prose format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. The chapter divides into three sections: the appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab as master craftsmen (vv. 1-11), the Sabbath commandment as covenant sign (vv. 12-17), and the giving of the stone tablets (v. 18). The chapter bridges the divine instructions (chs. 25-30) and their execution (chs. 35-40) by naming the human hands that will do the work — hands filled with *ruakh Elohim* ("the Spirit of God") — and then establishing the limit of that work: the Sabbath. The chapter closes with the most concrete object in the entire Sinai narrative: two tablets of stone, written by the finger of God. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 74-75) follows the MT sequence closely throughout, with the section beginning in the right column of page 74 marked with the section number KE (= 25) in LXX section numbering.

---

## The Appointment of Bezalel (vv. 1-5)

**31:1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**31:2** "See, I have called by name Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.

**31:3** And I have filled him with the Spirit of God, in wisdom and in understanding and in knowledge and in all workmanship,

**31:4** to devise designs, to work in gold and in silver and in bronze,

**31:5** and in cutting of stone for setting, and in cutting of wood, to work in all workmanship.

---

## The Appointment of Oholiab (vv. 6-11)

**31:6** And I — behold, I have given with him Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan; and in the heart of all the wise of heart I have put wisdom, and they shall make all that I have commanded you:

**31:7** the tent of meeting, and the ark for the testimony, and the mercy seat which is upon it, and all the vessels of the tent,

**31:8** and the table and its vessels, and the pure lampstand and all its vessels, and the altar of incense,

**31:9** and the altar of burnt offering and all its vessels, and the basin and its stand,

**31:10** and the woven garments, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, to serve as priests,

**31:11** and the oil of anointing, and the incense of fragrant spices for the holy place; according to all that I have commanded you they shall do."

---

## The Sabbath as Covenant Sign (vv. 12-17)

**31:12** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**31:13** "And you — speak to the Sons of Israel, saying: 'Surely my Sabbaths you shall keep, for it is a sign between me and between you throughout your generations, to know that I am YHWH who sanctifies you.

**31:14** And you shall keep the Sabbath, for it is holy to you; the one who profanes it shall surely be put to death, for everyone who does work on it — that soul shall be cut off from the midst of her people.

**31:15** Six days work shall be done, and on the seventh day is a Sabbath of complete rest, holy to YHWH; everyone who does work on the Sabbath day shall surely be put to death.

**31:16** And the Sons of Israel shall keep the Sabbath, to make the Sabbath throughout their generations — a covenant of perpetuity.

**31:17** Between me and between the Sons of Israel it is a sign for perpetuity, for in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth, and on the seventh day he ceased and he refreshed himself.'"

---

## The Tablets of the Testimony (v. 18)

**31:18** And he gave to Moses, when he finished speaking with him on Mount Sinai, the two tablets of the testimony — tablets of stone, written by the finger of God.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Structural Observation

Exodus 31 concludes the seven-chapter tabernacle instruction sequence (25-31), dividing into three sections of decreasing length but increasing theological weight: the appointment of the artisans (vv. 1-11), the Sabbath commandment (vv. 12-17), and the giving of the tablets (v. 18). The seven divine speeches of chapters 25-31 (at 25:1; 30:11; 30:17; 30:22; 30:34; 31:1; 31:12) correspond structurally to the seven days of creation, with the Sabbath command at 31:12-17 corresponding to the seventh day of rest. The verse counts mirror this theological architecture: the practical (who will build), the temporal (when they must stop), and the transcendent (the divine inscription that grounds the enterprise).

### Key Decisions

1. **"Spoke" for *va-yedabber* (v. 1) and *va-yomer* (v. 12):** The MT reads *va-yedabber* ("and he spoke") in v. 1 but *va-yomer* ("and he said") in v. 12. EMET correctly marks this distinction; LOGOS renders both as "spoke"; PNEUMA renders both as "said." The consensus renders both with "spoke" for consistency with the established divine speech formula across the SOPHIA Bible, while noting that the underlying Hebrew shifts from the formal *va-yedabber* to the more intimate *va-yomer* at v. 12, marking the transition from technical instruction to covenantal declaration.

2. **"Workmanship" for *melakhah* (v. 3):** EMET: "work." LOGOS: "workmanship." PNEUMA: "craftsmanship." The consensus follows LOGOS. "Workmanship" preserves the connection to the creation vocabulary (*melakhah* in Gen 2:2-3) while conveying the artisanal dimension more precisely than "work." PNEUMA's "craftsmanship" is acceptable but slightly narrower in range.

3. **"Cutting" for *kharoshet* (v. 5):** EMET uses "cutting" for both stone and wood. LOGOS uses "cutting" for stone and "carving" for wood. PNEUMA paraphrases freely. The consensus follows EMET: the Hebrew uses the same word *kharoshet* for both materials, and the translation should preserve this repetition rather than introducing a distinction the Hebrew does not make.

4. **"The wise of heart" for *khakham-lev* (v. 6):** EMET: "the wise of heart." LOGOS: "wise-hearted one." PNEUMA: "all who are naturally gifted." The consensus follows EMET. "The wise of heart" preserves the Hebrew construct and the centrality of *lev* ("heart") as the seat of skill and intelligence. PNEUMA's "naturally gifted" imports an interpretation absent from the Hebrew and obscures the key term *lev*.

5. **"To make the Sabbath" for *la'asot et-ha-shabbat* (v. 16):** EMET: "to make the Sabbath." LOGOS: "to observe the Sabbath." PNEUMA: "celebrating it." The consensus follows EMET. The verb *asah* ("to make, to do") is deliberate; Israel actively constructs rest as a positive act, rather than merely observing or celebrating it. This is one of the chapter's most distinctive expressions.

6. **"A covenant of perpetuity" for *berit olam* (v. 16):** EMET: "a covenant of perpetuity." LOGOS: "a covenant forever." PNEUMA: "a permanent covenant." The consensus follows EMET. "Of perpetuity" preserves *olam* as a noun in construct rather than flattening it to an adjective, consistent with the Hebrew syntax.

7. **"He ceased and he refreshed himself" for *shavat va-yinnafash* (v. 17):** EMET: "he ceased and he refreshed himself." LOGOS: "He ceased and was refreshed." PNEUMA: "he stopped and caught his breath." The consensus follows EMET. "He ceased" preserves the root *shavat* (from which *shabbat* derives). "He refreshed himself" captures the niphal reflexive of *nafash* (related to *nefesh*, "soul, breath, life") — an extraordinary anthropomorphism depicting YHWH as re-souled after the labor of creation. LOGOS's passive "was refreshed" loses the reflexive force. PNEUMA's "caught his breath" is vivid but colloquial for a consensus text, though its underlying insight into the anthropomorphism is sound.

8. **"Written" for *ketuvim* (v. 18):** EMET and LOGOS: "written." PNEUMA: "inscribed." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. "Written" directly renders the passive participle from *katav* ("to write"). PNEUMA's "inscribed" is defensible but introduces nuance not warranted by the Hebrew root.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text (pages 74-75) follows the MT closely. Notable features: (1) At v. 3, the LXX renders *ruakh Elohim* as *pneuma theion* ("divine spirit") rather than *pneuma tou theou* ("Spirit of God"), a subtle shift from personal to qualitative. (2) At v. 6, Vaticanus reads "Eliab" rather than "Oholiab," a consistent LXX variant possibly reflecting a different Hebrew Vorlage. (3) At v. 10, *bigdei ha-serad* is rendered *stolas tas leitourgikass* ("liturgical robes"), interpreting the obscure *serad* as service function. (4) At v. 17, *va-yinnafash* is rendered *katepausen* ("he rested/ceased"), flattening the MT's anthropomorphic "refreshed himself" — an apparent theological smoothing. (5) Throughout, YHWH is rendered *kurios* per standard LXX convention.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Ruling |
|-------|-------|-----------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| v. 1 vs. v. 12 | Speech formula verb | "spoke" (v.1) / "said" (v.12) — correctly distinguishes *va-yedabber* from *va-yomer* | "spoke" for both | "said" for both | "spoke" for both, with note on underlying Hebrew shift |
| v. 3 | *melakhah* | "work" | "workmanship" | "craftsmanship" | "workmanship" (follows LOGOS) |
| v. 5 | *kharoshet* for wood | "cutting" | "carving" | paraphrase | "cutting" (follows EMET; Hebrew uses same word for both materials) |
| v. 6 | *khakham-lev* | "the wise of heart" | "wise-hearted one" | "all who are naturally gifted" | "the wise of heart" (follows EMET) |
| v. 10 | *le-khahen* | "to be priests" | "to minister as priests" | "when they serve as priests" | "to serve as priests" (compromise) |
| v. 11 | *qetoret ha-sammim* | "incense of spices" | "incense of fragrant spices" | "fragrant incense" | "incense of fragrant spices" (follows LOGOS) |
| v. 13 | *akh* | "surely" | "surely" | "above all" | "surely" (follows EMET/LOGOS) |
| v. 16 | *la'asot et-ha-shabbat* | "to make the Sabbath" | "to observe the Sabbath" | "celebrating it" | "to make the Sabbath" (follows EMET) |
| v. 16 | *berit olam* | "a covenant of perpetuity" | "a covenant forever" | "a permanent covenant" | "a covenant of perpetuity" (follows EMET) |
| v. 17 | *le-olam* | "for perpetuity" | "forever" | "forever" | "for perpetuity" (follows EMET; consistent with *olam* rendering in v.16) |
| v. 17 | *shavat va-yinnafash* | "he ceased and he refreshed himself" | "He ceased and was refreshed" | "he stopped and caught his breath" | "he ceased and he refreshed himself" (follows EMET) |
| v. 18 | *ketuvim* | "written" | "written" | "inscribed" | "written" (follows EMET/LOGOS) |

### Summary of Divergence Patterns

**EMET prevails** in 8 of 12 disputed renderings. This chapter's content — theologically precise terms for divine endowment, Sabbath law, and covenant formulae — favors literal fidelity. EMET's commitment to preserving Hebrew word repetition (*kharoshet* used identically for stone and wood), construct phrases (*khakham-lev*, *berit olam*), and the distinctive verb *la'asot* ("to make") for Sabbath observance consistently produces the most transparent rendering of the Hebrew.

**LOGOS prevails** in 3 renderings: "workmanship" for *melakhah* (v. 3), "incense of fragrant spices" for *qetoret ha-sammim* (v. 11), and "to serve as priests" (v. 10, a compromise drawing on all three). LOGOS's formal register provides the right balance when EMET's ultra-literal rendering ("work") loses precision.

**PNEUMA** does not prevail in any disputed rendering for this chapter. Its dynamic renderings — "above all" for *akh*, "naturally gifted" for *khakham-lev*, "caught his breath" for *va-yinnafash*, "celebrating" for *la'asot* — are insightful and often capture the theological spirit of the text, but they introduce interpretive specificity that a consensus translation should leave to the reader. PNEUMA's translator notes, however, are consistently excellent and have informed several of SOPHIA's council notes.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
